name: sqladmin

metadata.name: sqladmin
metadata.summary: SQLAlchemy admin for FastAPI and Starlette

documentation: --- # SQLAlchemy Admin for Starlette/FastAPI SQLAdmin is a flexible Admin interface for SQLAlchemy models. Main features include: * [SQLAlchemy](https://github.com/sqlalchemy/sqlalchemy) sync/async engines * [Starlette](https://github.com/encode/starlette) integration * [FastAPI](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi) integration * [WTForms](https://github.com/wtforms/wtforms) form building * [SQLModel](https://github.com/tiangolo/sqlmodel) support * UI using [Tabler](https://github.com/tabler/tabler) --- **Documentation**: [https://aminalaee.dev/sqladmin](https://aminalaee.dev/sqladmin) **Source Code**: [https://github.com/aminalaee/sqladmin](https://github.com/aminalaee/sqladmin) **Online Demo**: [Demo](https://sqladmin-demo.aminalaee.dev/admin/) --- ## Installation Install using `pip`: ```shell $ pip install sqladmin ``` This will install the full version of sqladmin with optional dependencies: ```shell $ pip install "sqladmin[full]" ``` --- ## Screenshots ## Quickstart Let's define an example SQLAlchemy model: ```python from sqlalchemy import Column, Integer, String, create_engine from sqlalchemy.orm import declarative_base Base = declarative_base() engine = create_engine( "sqlite:///example.db", connect_args={"check_same_thread": False}, ) class User(Base): __tablename__ = "users" id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True) name = Column(String) Base.metadata.create_all(engine) # Create tables ``` If you want to use `SQLAdmin` with `FastAPI`: ```python from fastapi import FastAPI from sqladmin import Admin, ModelView app = FastAPI() admin = Admin(app, engine) class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User): column_list = [User.id, User.name] admin.add_view(UserAdmin) ``` Or if you want to use `SQLAdmin` with `Starlette`: ```python from sqladmin import Admin, ModelView from starlette.applications import Starlette app = Starlette() admin = Admin(app, engine) class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User): column_list = [User.id, User.name] admin.add_view(UserAdmin) ``` Now visiting `/admin` on your browser you can see the `SQLAdmin` interface. ## Related projects and inspirations * [Flask-Admin](https://github.com/flask-admin/flask-admin) Admin interface for Flask supporting different database backends and ORMs. This project has inspired SQLAdmin extensively and most of the features and configurations are implemented the same. * [FastAPI-Admin](https://github.com/fastapi-admin/fastapi-admin) Admin interface for FastAPI which works with `TortoiseORM`. * [Dashboard](https://github.com/encode/dashboard) Admin interface for ASGI frameworks which works with the `orm` package.

functions.name: sqladmin.helpers.secure_filename
documentation:
Ported from Werkzeug.

Pass it a filename and it will return a secure version of it. This
filename can then safely be stored on a regular file system and passed
to {func}`os.path.join`. The filename returned is an ASCII only string
for maximum portability.
On windows systems the function also makes sure that the file is not
named after one of the special device files.

functions.name: sqladmin.helpers.stream_to_csv
documentation:
Function that takes a callable (that yields from a CSV Writer), and
provides it a writer that streams the output directly instead of
storing it in a buffer. The direct output stream is intended to go
inside a `starlette.responses.StreamingResponse`.

Loosely adapted from here:

https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.8/howto/outputting-csv/

functions.name: sqladmin.helpers.get_object_identifier
documentation: Returns a value that uniquely identifies this object.

classes.name: sqladmin.helpers.Writer
documentation: https://docs.python.org/3/library/csv.html#writer-objects

classes.name: sqladmin.helpers._PseudoBuffer
documentation:
An object that implements just the write method of the file-like
interface.

functions.name: sqladmin.application.expose
documentation: Expose View with information.

functions.name: sqladmin.application.action
documentation:
Decorate a [`ModelView`][sqladmin.models.ModelView] function
with this to:

* expose it as a custom "action" route
* add a button to the admin panel to invoke the action

When invoked from the admin panel, the following query parameter(s) are passed:

* `pks`: the comma-separated list of selected object PKs - can be empty

Args:
    name: Unique name for the action - should be alphanumeric, dash and underscore
    label: Human-readable text describing action
    confirmation_message: Message to show before confirming action
    include_in_schema: Indicating if the endpoint be included in the schema
    add_in_detail: Indicating if action should be dispalyed on model detail page
    add_in_list: Indicating if action should be dispalyed on model list page

classes.name: sqladmin.application.BaseAdmin

methods.name: sqladmin.application.BaseAdmin.views
documentation:
Get list of ModelView and BaseView instances lazily.

Returns:
    List of ModelView and BaseView instances added to Admin.

methods.name: sqladmin.application.BaseAdmin.add_view
documentation:
Add ModelView or BaseView classes to Admin.
This is a shortcut that will handle both `add_model_view` and `add_base_view`.

methods.name: sqladmin.application.BaseAdmin.add_model_view
documentation:
Add ModelView to the Admin.

???+ usage
    ```python
    from sqladmin import Admin, ModelView

    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        pass

    admin.add_model_view(UserAdmin)
    ```

methods.name: sqladmin.application.BaseAdmin.add_base_view
documentation:
Add BaseView to the Admin.

???+ usage
    ```python
    from sqladmin import BaseView, expose

    class CustomAdmin(BaseView):
        name = "Custom Page"
        icon = "fa-solid fa-chart-line"

        @expose("/custom", methods=["GET"])
        async def test_page(self, request: Request):
            return await self.templates.TemplateResponse(request, "custom.html")

    admin.add_base_view(CustomAdmin)
    ```

Base class for implementing Admin interface.

Danger:
    This class should almost never be used directly.

classes.name: sqladmin.application.BaseAdminView
documentation: Manage right to access to an action from a model

classes.name: sqladmin.application.Admin

methods.name: sqladmin.application.Admin.__init__
documentation:
Args:
    app: Starlette or FastAPI application.
    engine: SQLAlchemy engine instance.
    session_maker: SQLAlchemy sessionmaker instance.
    base_url: Base URL for Admin interface.
    title: Admin title.
    logo_url: URL of logo to be displayed instead of title.
    favicon_url: URL of favicon to be displayed.

methods.name: sqladmin.application.Admin.index
documentation: Index route which can be overridden to create dashboards.

methods.name: sqladmin.application.Admin.list
documentation: List route to display paginated Model instances.

methods.name: sqladmin.application.Admin.details
documentation: Details route.

methods.name: sqladmin.application.Admin.delete
documentation: Delete route.

methods.name: sqladmin.application.Admin.create
documentation: Create model endpoint.

methods.name: sqladmin.application.Admin.edit
documentation: Edit model endpoint.

methods.name: sqladmin.application.Admin.export
documentation: Export model endpoint.

methods.name: sqladmin.application.Admin.ajax_lookup
documentation: Ajax lookup route.

methods.name: sqladmin.application.Admin.get_save_redirect_url
documentation:
Get the redirect URL after a save action
which is triggered from create/edit page.

methods.name: sqladmin.application.Admin._handle_form_data
documentation:
Handle form data and modify in case of UploadFile.
This is needed since in edit page
there's no way to show current file of object.

Main entrypoint to admin interface.

???+ usage
    ```python
    from fastapi import FastAPI
    from sqladmin import Admin, ModelView

    from mymodels import User # SQLAlchemy model


    app = FastAPI()
    admin = Admin(app, engine)


    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        column_list = [User.id, User.name]


    admin.add_view(UserAdmin)
    ```

classes.name: sqladmin.fields.DateField
documentation: Add custom DatePickerWidget for data-format and data-date-format fields

classes.name: sqladmin.fields.DateTimeField
documentation: Allows modifying the datetime format of a DateTimeField using form_args.

classes.name: sqladmin.fields.IntervalField
documentation: A text field which stores a `datetime.timedelta` object.

classes.name: sqladmin.fields.QuerySelectMultipleField
documentation:
Very similar to QuerySelectField with the difference that this will
display a multiple select. The data property will hold a list with ORM
model instances and will be an empty list when no value is selected.

If any of the items in the data list or submitted form data cannot be
found in the query, this will result in a validation error.

classes.name: sqladmin.fields.FileField
documentation: File field which is clearable.

classes.name: sqladmin._validators.CurrencyValidator
documentation: Form validator for sqlalchemy_utils CurrencyType.

classes.name: sqladmin._validators.PhoneNumberValidator
documentation: Form validator for sqlalchemy_utils PhoneNumberType.

classes.name: sqladmin._validators.ColorValidator
documentation: General Color validator using `colour` package.

classes.name: sqladmin._validators.TimezoneValidator
documentation: Form validator for sqlalchemy_utils TimezoneType.

functions.name: sqladmin.formatters.empty_formatter
documentation: Return empty string for `None` value

functions.name: sqladmin.formatters.bool_formatter
documentation: Return check icon if value is `True` or X otherwise.

classes.name: sqladmin.widgets.DatePickerWidget
documentation: Date picker widget.

classes.name: sqladmin.widgets.DateTimePickerWidget
documentation: Datetime picker widget.

classes.name: sqladmin.widgets.FileInputWidget
documentation: File input widget with clear checkbox.

functions.name: sqladmin.authentication.login_required
documentation:
Decorator to check authentication of Admin routes.
If no authentication backend is setup, this will do nothing.

classes.name: sqladmin.authentication.AuthenticationBackend

methods.name: sqladmin.authentication.AuthenticationBackend.login
documentation:
Implement login logic here.
You can access the login form data `await request.form()`
andvalidate the credentials.

methods.name: sqladmin.authentication.AuthenticationBackend.logout
documentation:
Implement logout logic here.
This will usually clear the session with `request.session.clear()`.

methods.name: sqladmin.authentication.AuthenticationBackend.authenticate
documentation:
Implement authenticate logic here.
This method will be called for each incoming request
to validate the authentication.

If a 'Response' or `RedirectResponse` is returned,
that response is returned to the user,
otherwise a True/False is expected.

Base class for implementing the Authentication into SQLAdmin.
You need to inherit this class and override the methods:
`login`, `logout` and `authenticate`.

modules.name: sqladmin.forms
documentation: The converters are from Flask-Admin project.

classes.name: sqladmin.models.ModelViewMeta
documentation:
Metaclass used to specify class variables in ModelView.

Danger:
    This class should almost never be used directly.

methods.name: sqladmin.models.BaseModelView.is_visible
documentation:
Override this method if you want dynamically
hide or show administrative views from SQLAdmin menu structure
By default, item is visible in menu.
Both is_visible and is_accessible to be displayed in menu.

methods.name: sqladmin.models.BaseModelView.is_accessible
documentation:
Override this method to add permission checks.
SQLAdmin does not make any assumptions about the authentication system
used in your application, so it is up to you to implement it.
By default, it will allow access for everyone.

classes.name: sqladmin.models.BaseView

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.BaseView.name
documentation: Name of the view to be displayed.

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.BaseView.identity
documentation: Same as name but it will be used for URL of the endpoints.

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.BaseView.methods
documentation:
List of method names for the endpoint.
By default it's set to `["GET"]` only.

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.BaseView.include_in_schema
documentation:
Control whether this endpoint
should be included in the schema.

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.BaseView.icon
documentation:
Display icon for ModelAdmin in the sidebar.
Currently only supports FontAwesome and Tabler icons.

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.BaseView.category
documentation: Category name to group views together.

Base class for defining admnistrative views for the model.

???+ usage
    ```python
    from sqladmin import BaseView, expose

    class CustomAdmin(BaseView):
        name = "Custom Page"
        icon = "fa-solid fa-chart-line"

        @expose("/custom", methods=["GET"])
        async def test_page(self, request: Request):
            return await self.templates.TemplateResponse(request, "custom.html")

    admin.add_base_view(CustomAdmin)
    ```

classes.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView

methods.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.get_list_value
documentation: Get tuple of (value, formatted_value) for the list view.

methods.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.get_detail_value
documentation: Get tuple of (value, formatted_value) for the detail view.

methods.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView._build_column_list
documentation:
This function generalizes constructing a list of columns
for any sequence of inclusions or exclusions.

methods.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.get_list_columns
documentation: Get list of properties to display in List page.

methods.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.get_details_columns
documentation: Get list of properties to display in Detail page.

methods.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.get_form_columns
documentation: Get list of properties to display in the form.

methods.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.get_export_columns
documentation: Get list of properties to export.

methods.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.on_model_change
documentation:
Perform some actions before a model is created or updated.
By default does nothing.

methods.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.after_model_change
documentation:
Perform some actions after a model was created
or updated and committed to the database.
By default does nothing.

methods.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.on_model_delete
documentation:
Perform some actions before a model is deleted.
By default does nothing.

methods.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.after_model_delete
documentation:
Perform some actions after a model is deleted.
By default do nothing.

methods.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.search_placeholder
documentation:
Return search placeholder text.

???+ example
    ```python
    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        column_labels = dict(name="Name", email="Email")
        column_searchable_list = [User.name, User.email]

    # placeholder is: "Name, Email"
    ```

methods.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.search_query
documentation:
Specify the search query given the SQLAlchemy statement
and term to search for.
It can be used for doing more complex queries like JSON objects. For example:

```py
return stmt.filter(MyModel.name == term)
```

methods.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.list_query
documentation:
The SQLAlchemy select expression used for the list page which can be customized.
By default it will select all objects without any filters.

methods.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.form_edit_query
documentation:
The SQLAlchemy select expression used for the edit form page which can be
customized. By default it will select the object by primary key(s) without any
additional filters.

methods.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.count_query
documentation:
The SQLAlchemy select expression used for the count query
which can be customized.
By default it will select all objects without any filters.

methods.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.sort_query
documentation:
A method that is called every time the fields are sorted
and that can be customized.
By default, sorting takes place by default fields.

The 'sortBy' and 'sort' query parameters are available in this request context.

methods.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.get_export_name
documentation: The file name when exporting.

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.name_plural
documentation:
Plural name of ModelView.
Default value is Model class name + `s`.

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.can_create
documentation: Permission for creating new Models. Default value is set to `True`.

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.can_edit
documentation: Permission for editing Models. Default value is set to `True`.

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.can_delete
documentation: Permission for deleting Models. Default value is set to `True`.

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.can_view_details
documentation:
Permission for viewing full details of Models.
Default value is set to `True`.

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.can_export
documentation:
Permission for exporting lists of Models.
Default value is set to `True`.

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.column_list
documentation:
List of columns to display in `List` page.
Columns can either be string names or SQLAlchemy columns.

???+ note
    By default only Model primary key is displayed.

???+ example
    ```python
    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        column_list = [User.id, User.name]
    ```

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.column_exclude_list
documentation:
List of columns to exclude in `List` page.
Columns can either be string names or SQLAlchemy columns.

???+ example
    ```python
    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        column_exclude_list = [User.id, User.name]
    ```

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.column_formatters
documentation:
Dictionary of list view column formatters.
Columns can either be string names or SQLAlchemy columns.

???+ example
    ```python
    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        column_formatters = {User.name: lambda m, a: m.name[:10]}
    ```

The format function has the prototype:
???+ formatter
    ```python
    def formatter(model, attribute):
        # `model` is model instance
        # `attribute` is a Union[ColumnProperty, RelationshipProperty]
        pass
    ```

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.page_size
documentation:
Default number of items to display in `List` page pagination.
Default value is set to `10`.

???+ example
    ```python
    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        page_size = 25
    ```

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.page_size_options
documentation:
Pagination choices displayed in `List` page.
Default value is set to `[10, 25, 50, 100]`.

???+ example
    ```python
    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        page_size_options = [50, 100]
    ```

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.column_searchable_list
documentation:
A collection of the searchable columns.
It is assumed that only text-only fields are searchable,
but it is up to the model implementation to decide.

???+ example
    ```python
    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        column_searchable_list = [User.name]
    ```

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.column_sortable_list
documentation:
Collection of the sortable columns for the list view.

???+ example
    ```python
    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        column_sortable_list = [User.name]
    ```

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.column_default_sort
documentation:
Default sort column if no sorting is applied.

???+ example
    ```python
    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        column_default_sort = "email"
    ```

You can use tuple to control ascending descending order. In following example, items
will be sorted in descending order:

???+ example
    ```python
    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        column_default_sort = ("email", True)
    ```

If you want to sort by more than one column, you can pass a list of tuples

???+ example
    ```python
    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        column_default_sort = [("email", True), ("name", False)]
    ```

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.column_details_list
documentation:
List of columns to display in `Detail` page.
Columns can either be string names or SQLAlchemy columns.

???+ note
    By default all columns of Model are displayed.

???+ example
    ```python
    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        column_details_list = [User.id, User.name, User.mail]
    ```

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.column_details_exclude_list
documentation:
List of columns to exclude from displaying in `Detail` page.
Columns can either be string names or SQLAlchemy columns.

???+ example
    ```python
    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        column_details_exclude_list = [User.mail]
    ```

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.column_formatters_detail
documentation:
Dictionary of details view column formatters.
Columns can either be string names or SQLAlchemy columns.

???+ example
    ```python
    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        column_formatters_detail = {User.name: lambda m, a: m.name[:10]}
    ```

The format function has the prototype:
???+ formatter
    ```python
    def formatter(model, attribute):
        # `model` is model instance
        # `attribute` is a Union[ColumnProperty, RelationshipProperty]
        pass
    ```

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.save_as
documentation:
Set `save_as` to enable a “save as new” feature on admin change forms.

Normally, objects have three save options:
``Save`, `Save and continue editing` and `Save and add another`.

If save_as is True, `Save and add another` will be replaced
by a `Save as new` button
that creates a new object (with a new ID)
rather than updating the existing object.

By default, `save_as` is set to `False`.

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.save_as_continue
documentation:
When `save_as=True`, the default redirect after saving the new object
is to the edit view for that object.
If you set `save_as_continue=False`, the redirect will be to the list view.

By default, `save_as_continue` is set to `True`.

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.list_template
documentation: List view template. Default is `sqladmin/list.html`.

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.create_template
documentation: Create view template. Default is `sqladmin/create.html`.

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.details_template
documentation: Details view template. Default is `sqladmin/details.html`.

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.edit_template
documentation: Edit view template. Default is `sqladmin/edit.html`.

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.column_export_list
documentation:
List of columns to include when exporting.
Columns can either be string names or SQLAlchemy columns.

???+ example
    ```python
    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        column_export_list = [User.id, User.name]
    ```

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.column_export_exclude_list
documentation:
List of columns to exclude when exporting.
Columns can either be string names or SQLAlchemy columns.

???+ example
    ```python
    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        column_export_exclude_list = [User.id, User.name]
    ```

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.export_types
documentation:
A list of available export filetypes.
Currently only `csv` is supported.

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.export_max_rows
documentation:
Maximum number of rows allowed for export.
Unlimited by default.

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.form
documentation:
Form class.
Override if you want to use custom form for your model.
Will completely disable form scaffolding functionality.

???+ example
    ```python
    class MyForm(Form):
        name = StringField('Name')

    class MyModelView(ModelView, model=User):
        form = MyForm
    ```

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.form_base_class
documentation:
Base form class.
Will be used by form scaffolding function when creating model form.
Useful if you want to have custom constructor or override some fields.

???+ example
    ```python
    class MyBaseForm(Form):
        def do_something(self):
            pass

    class MyModelView(ModelView, model=User):
        form_base_class = MyBaseForm
    ```

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.form_args
documentation:
Dictionary of form field arguments.
Refer to WTForms documentation for list of possible options.

???+ example
    ```python
    from wtforms.validators import DataRequired

    class MyModelView(ModelView, model=User):
        form_args = dict(
            name=dict(label="User Name", validators=[DataRequired()])
        )
    ```

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.form_widget_args
sqladmin.models.ModelView.form_columns
documentation:
List of columns to include in the form.
Columns can either be string names or SQLAlchemy columns.

???+ note
    By default all columns of Model are included in the form.

???+ example
    ```python
    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        form_columns = [User.name, User.mail]
    ```

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.form_excluded_columns
documentation:
List of columns to exclude from the form.
Columns can either be string names or SQLAlchemy columns.

???+ example
    ```python
    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        form_excluded_columns = [User.id]
    ```

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.form_overrides
documentation:
Dictionary of form column overrides.

???+ example
    ```python
    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        form_overrides = dict(name=wtf.FileField)
    ```

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.form_include_pk
documentation:
Control if form should include primary key columns or not.

???+ example
    ```python
    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        form_include_pk = True
    ```

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.form_ajax_refs
sqladmin.models.ModelView.form_converter
documentation:
Custom form converter class.
Useful if you want to add custom form conversion in addition to the defaults.

???+ example
    ```python
    class PhoneNumberConverter(ModelConverter):
        pass

    class UserAdmin(ModelAdmin, model=User):
        form_converter = PhoneNumberConverter
    ```

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.form_rules
documentation:
List of rendering rules for model creation and edit form.
This property changes default form rendering behavior and to rearrange
order of rendered fields, add some text between fields, group them, etc.
If not set, will use default Flask-Admin form rendering logic.

???+ example
    ```python
    class UserAdmin(ModelAdmin, model=User):
        form_rules = [
            "first_name",
            "last_name",
        ]
    ```

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.form_create_rules
documentation: Customized rules for the create form. Cannot be specified with `form_rules`.

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.form_edit_rules
documentation: Customized rules for the edit form. Cannot be specified with `form_rules`.

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.column_labels
documentation:
A mapping of column labels, used to map column names to new names.
Dictionary keys can be string names or SQLAlchemy columns with string values.

???+ example
    ```python
    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        column_labels = {User.mail: "Email"}
    ```

class_variables.name: sqladmin.models.ModelView.column_type_formatters
documentation:
Dictionary of value type formatters to be used in the list view.

By default, two types are formatted:

    - None will be displayed as an empty string
    - bool will be displayed as a checkmark if it is True otherwise as an X.

If you don’t like the default behavior and don’t want any type formatters applied,
just override this property with an empty dictionary:

???+ example
    ```python
    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        column_type_formatters = dict()
    ```

Base class for defining admnistrative behaviour for the model.

???+ usage
    ```python
    from sqladmin import ModelView

    from mymodels import User # SQLAlchemy model

    class UserAdmin(ModelView, model=User):
        can_create = True
    ```
